title: Gwent (county)

infobox.value: [Blaenau Gwent](/wiki/Blaenau_Gwent "Blaenau Gwent") [Caerphilly](/wiki/Caerphilly_County_Borough "Caerphilly County Borough") [Monmouthshire](/wiki/Monmouthshire "Monmouthshire") [Newport](/wiki/Newport,_Wales "Newport, Wales") [Torfaen](/wiki/Torfaen "Torfaen") Preserved county of Gwent
[Shire Hall, Newport](/wiki/Shire_Hall,_Newport "Shire Hall, Newport") (1974–1978) [County Hall, Cwmbran](/wiki/County_Hall,_Cwmbran "County Hall, Cwmbran") (1978–1996)

infobox.key: [Authority control databases](/wiki/Help:Authority_control "Help:Authority control") [Edit this at Wikidata](https://www.wikidata.org/wiki/Q1376956#identifiers "Edit this at Wikidata")

infobox.value: is a [preserved county](/wiki/Preserved_counties_of_Wales "Preserved counties of Wales") and former local government county in southeast [Wales](/wiki/Wales "Wales"). A county of Gwent was formed on 1 April 1974, under the [Local Government Act 1972](/wiki/Local_Government_Act_1972 "Local Government Act 1972"); it was named after the ancient [Kingdom of Gwent](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Gwent "Kingdom of Gwent"). The authority was a successor to both the [administrative county](/wiki/Administrative_counties_of_Wales "Administrative counties of Wales") of [Monmouthshire](/wiki/Monmouthshire_(historic) "Monmouthshire (historic)") (with minor boundary changes)[[2]](#cite_note-2) and the [county borough](/wiki/County_borough "County borough") of [Newport](/wiki/Newport,_Wales "Newport, Wales") (both authorities which were legally part of [England](/wiki/England "England") until the Act came into force[[3]](#cite_note-3)[[4]](#cite_note-4) although [considered jointly](/wiki/Wales_and_Monmouthshire "Wales and Monmouthshire") with Wales for certain purposes).[[5]](#cite_note-GwentCountyHistory-5)[[6]](#cite_note-GwentLocalHistory-6)

Less the parish of [St Mellons](/wiki/St_Mellons "St Mellons") and urban districts of [Bedwas and Machen](/wiki/Bedwas_and_Machen "Bedwas and Machen"), [Rhymney](/wiki/Rhymney "Rhymney") and part of [Bedwellty](/wiki/Bedwellty "Bedwellty") and the addition of the parishes of [Llanelly](/wiki/Llanelly "Llanelly") and [Brynmawr](/wiki/Brynmawr "Brynmawr").
Local Government Act 1933 c. 51, First Schedule, Part I (a) Administrative Counties in England: Monmouth; Part II (a) County Boroughs in England: Newport.

Local Government Act 1972 c. 70, s. 1 New local government areas in England, ss. 12 "In this section 'England' does not include the administrative county of Monmouthshire or the county borough of Newport." & s. 20 New local government areas in Wales, ss. 7 "In this section 'Wales' includes the administrative county of Monmouthshire and the county borough of Newport."

[Williams, Chris](/wiki/Chris_Williams_(academic) "Chris Williams (academic)") (2011). "The Question of Monmouthshire". In Williams, Chris; Williams, Sian Rhiannon (eds.). *Industrial Monmouthshire 1780-1914*. The Gwent County History. Vol. 4. Cardiff: University of Wales Press. p. 357. [ISBN](/wiki/ISBN_(identifier) "ISBN (identifier)") [978-0-7083-2365-6](/wiki/Special:BookSources/978-0-7083-2365-6 "Special:BookSources/978-0-7083-2365-6"). [OCLC](/wiki/OCLC_(identifier) "OCLC (identifier)") [56570946](https://search.worldcat.org/oclc/56570946).

Hopkins, Tony (Summer 1991). ["In search of Monmouthshire, 1536-1972"](https://journals.library.wales/view/1337678/1339197/9). *Gwent Local History* (71): 8–12. Retrieved 26 March 2018 – via [Welsh Journals Online](/wiki/Welsh_Journals_Online "Welsh Journals Online") at the [National Library of Wales](/wiki/National_Library_of_Wales "National Library of Wales").

["The Preserved Counties (Amendment to Boundaries) (Wales) Order 2003"](http://www.opsi.gov.uk/legislation/wales/wsi2003/20030974e.htm). [Office of Public Sector Information](/wiki/Office_of_Public_Sector_Information "Office of Public Sector Information"). 2003-04-01. Retrieved 2010-09-11.

2007 population estimate, calculated using 2003 borders for Blaenau Gwent, Caerphilly, Monmouthshire, Newport, Torfaen. Source: ["Table 10: Mid-2006 to Mid-2007 Population Estimates, Components of population change for local authorities in the United Kingdom"](https://web.archive.org/web/20110728154121/http://www.statistics.gov.uk/downloads/theme_population/Mid_2007_UK_England_%26_Wales_Scotland_and_Northern_Ireland%20_21_08_08.zip). [Office for National Statistics](/wiki/Office_for_National_Statistics "Office for National Statistics"). Archived from [the original](http://www.statistics.gov.uk/downloads/theme_population/Mid_2007_UK_England_&_Wales_Scotland_and_Northern_Ireland%20_21_08_08.zip) on 2011-07-28. Retrieved 2010-09-11.

From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia

Preserved county in south-east Wales

**Gwent** is a [preserved county](/wiki/Preserved_counties_of_Wales "Preserved counties of Wales") and former local government county in southeast [Wales](/wiki/Wales "Wales"). A county of Gwent was formed on 1 April 1974, under the [Local Government Act 1972](/wiki/Local_Government_Act_1972 "Local Government Act 1972"); it was named after the ancient [Kingdom of Gwent](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Gwent "Kingdom of Gwent"). The authority was a successor to both the [administrative county](/wiki/Administrative_counties_of_Wales "Administrative counties of Wales") of [Monmouthshire](/wiki/Monmouthshire_(historic) "Monmouthshire (historic)") (with minor boundary changes)[[2]](#cite_note-2) and the [county borough](/wiki/County_borough "County borough") of [Newport](/wiki/Newport,_Wales "Newport, Wales") (both authorities which were legally part of [England](/wiki/England "England") until the Act came into force[[3]](#cite_note-3)[[4]](#cite_note-4) although [considered jointly](/wiki/Wales_and_Monmouthshire "Wales and Monmouthshire") with Wales for certain purposes).[[5]](#cite_note-GwentCountyHistory-5)[[6]](#cite_note-GwentLocalHistory-6)

Under the [Local Government (Wales) Act 1994](/wiki/Local_Government_(Wales)_Act_1994 "Local Government (Wales) Act 1994"), the county of Gwent was abolished on 1 April 1996.[[7]](#cite_note-7) However, the name remains in use for one of the [preserved counties of Wales](/wiki/Preserved_counties_of_Wales "Preserved counties of Wales") for the ceremonial purposes of [Lieutenancy](/wiki/Lieutenancy_area "Lieutenancy area") and [High Shrievalty](/wiki/High_Sheriff "High Sheriff"), and its name also survives in various titles, e.g. [Gwent Police](/wiki/Gwent_Police "Gwent Police"), [Royal Gwent Hospital](/wiki/Royal_Gwent_Hospital "Royal Gwent Hospital"), [Gwent Wildlife Trust](/wiki/Gwent_Wildlife_Trust "Gwent Wildlife Trust") and [Coleg Gwent](/wiki/Coleg_Gwent "Coleg Gwent"). "Gwent" is often used as a synonym for the [historic county](/wiki/Historic_counties_of_Wales "Historic counties of Wales") of Monmouthshire – for example the Gwent Family History Society describes itself as "The key to roots in the historic county of Monmouthshire".[[8]](#cite_note-8)

The former administrative county was divided into several districts: [Blaenau Gwent](/wiki/Blaenau_Gwent "Blaenau Gwent"), [Islwyn](/wiki/Islwyn "Islwyn"), [Monmouth](/wiki/District_of_Monmouth "District of Monmouth"), [Newport](/wiki/District_of_Newport "District of Newport") and [Torfaen](/wiki/Torfaen "Torfaen"). The successor [unitary authorities](/wiki/Principal_areas_of_Wales "Principal areas of Wales") are [Blaenau Gwent](/wiki/Blaenau_Gwent "Blaenau Gwent"), [Caerphilly](/wiki/Caerphilly_County_Borough "Caerphilly County Borough") (part of which came from [Mid Glamorgan](/wiki/Mid_Glamorgan "Mid Glamorgan")), [Monmouthshire](/wiki/Monmouthshire "Monmouthshire") (which covers the eastern 60% of the [historic county of the same name](/wiki/Monmouthshire_(historic) "Monmouthshire (historic)")), [Newport](/wiki/Newport,_Wales "Newport, Wales") and [Torfaen](/wiki/Torfaen "Torfaen").

In 2003 the preserved county of Gwent expanded to include the whole of [Caerphilly County Borough](/wiki/Caerphilly_County_Borough "Caerphilly County Borough");[[9]](#cite_note-9) the Gwent Police area had already been realigned to these boundaries in 1996. In 2007, the population of this enlarged area was estimated as 560,500,[[10]](#cite_note-10) making it the most populous of the preserved counties of Wales.
